feedingProfile.preferredNpk: high-nitrogen, low/no-phosphorus (e.g. 30-0-10 or 21-0-0) before and at shoot emergence; stop nitrogen by early May

commonIssues: Overwatering and poorly drained soil cause bulb rot and fungal disease; garlic prefers consistent but not soggy moisture.
Stop watering about two weeks before harvest to avoid staining bulb wrappers and disease.
Critical bulbing period (mid-spring to early summer) needs steady moisture; drying out then reduces bulb size.
Avoid applying nitrogen after the first week of May (per UMN); late nitrogen delays bulbing.
Container minimum (15 L) assumes a pot at least 20 cm / 8 in deep for a small cluster of cloves; deeper/wider pots give larger bulbs and hold more plants.

Hardneck types are hardier than softneck; the cold-hardy minimum cited reflects RHS H4 (-10 to -5 C) and is conservative for well-mulched hardneck garlic, which survives much colder Minnesota winters under mulch.

source.citation: University of Minnesota Extension — Growing garlic in home gardens. Planting depth 2-3 in, cloves ~6 in apart, plant in fall after first killing frost; water at least 1 inch per week, stop two weeks before harvest; soil pH 6.0-7.0; moderate-to-high nitrogen, top-dress at shoot emergence and 2-3 weeks later, avoid nitrogen after first week of May (examples 30-0-10, 24-0-15); harvest late June to late July.

University of Minnesota Extension — Growing garlic in home gardens. Nitrogen feeding: top-dress as soon as shoots emerge then again 2-3 weeks afterward; low/no-phosphorus high-nitrogen products (30-0-10, 24-0-15); avoid nitrogen after first week in May.

University of Minnesota Extension — Growing garlic in home gardens. Soil pH between 6.0 and 7.0; well-drained fertile soil; planting depth 2-3 inches; fall planting one to two weeks after first killing frost.

Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC — Onion, Leek, Shallot & Garlic. Garlic grows best in full sun; soil pH 6.2-6.5; consistent soil moisture especially critical during bulbing; planted in fall; pre-plant 5-10-10 plus sulfur, followed by calcium nitrate (15.5-0-0) side-dressings through winter into spring; harvest when leaves yellow in early summer.
Clemson Cooperative Extension HGIC — Onion, Leek, Shallot & Garlic. Spacing 3-5 inches apart, 12-24 inches between rows; consistent moisture critical during the bulbing period.

Royal Horticultural Society (RHS) — Allium sativum (garlic). Position full sun; hardiness H4, minimum temperature -10 to -5 C; well-drained soil (chalk, loam, sand), alkaline to neutral; harvest from early summer once leaves turn yellow; cloves 15 cm apart, tip 2.5 cm below surface, rows 30 cm apart.

Utah State University Extension — Garlic in the Garden. Plant largest cloves pointed-end up 2-3 inches deep; space 3-4 inches apart, rows 6-10 inches apart; moisten soil thoroughly to a depth of 12 inches; shallow roots make consistent watering critical; side-dress nitrogen (21-0-0) in mid-April and late May.

Confidence note: targetSunHours (8 h) and minSunHours (6 h) are derived from the universal extension/RHS recommendation of 'full sun' (no source gives an exact hour count); 6+ h is the standard horticultural definition of full sun. baseIntervalDays of 4 is a conservative interpretation of UMN's '1 inch/week thorough soaking' and the bulbing-period 'every 3-5 days' guidance (no single canonical interval published). heatStressC (30 C) and the container minimum (15 L) are conservative estimates: garlic bulbing is impaired by high summer heat per multiple extensions, and no .edu source publishes an exact heat-stress threshold or a precise minimum container volume in liters. coldHardyMinC uses the conservative RHS H4 floor (-5 to -10 C) even though mulched hardneck garlic survives colder; field-grown Minnesota garlic tolerates well below this.
